subject: Urgent: Your account will be suspended in 24 hours
content:
Dear Customer,

We detected unusual activity on your account. Your account will be SUSPENDED within 24 hours unless you verify your identity immediately.

Click here to verify now: http://secure-paypa1-verify.com/login

Failure to act will result in permanent loss of access.

PayPal Security Team

redFlags.note: Domain uses a '1' instead of 'l' — a classic lookalike domain trick.
Artificial urgency pressures you to act before thinking carefully.
Real companies usually use your actual name, not a generic greeting.
The link domain doesn't match PayPal's real domain.

explanation: This is a classic credential-phishing email. The lookalike domain, urgent threat, and mismatched link are all textbook signs of an attack designed to steal your login details.

subject: [GitHub] Your password was changed
content:
Hi alex-dev,

Your GitHub password was changed on June 20, 2026 at 14:32 UTC.

If you made this change, no further action is needed.

If you did not make this change, please reset your password immediately and contact GitHub Support.

Thanks,
The GitHub Team
explanation: This is a legitimate security notification. It addresses the user by their actual username, doesn't ask you to click an urgent link, gives a calm and specific timestamp, and offers a safe next step (reset password directly through the official site) rather than an embedded link demanding immediate action.

subject: Delivery Notification
content: USPS: Your package could not be delivered due to an incomplete address. Update your delivery details within 12 hours to avoid return: usps-redelivery.info/track

redFlags.note: USPS does not use .info domains — their real domain is usps.com.
Tight artificial deadlines are a pressure tactic.
Unexpected delivery texts with links are one of the most common smishing patterns.

explanation: This is a smishing (SMS phishing) attempt impersonating a delivery service. The non-official domain and urgency are designed to get you to enter personal or payment information on a fake tracking page.

sender: Unknown Caller — claims to be 'Microsoft Support'
subject: Vishing Call Transcript
content: "Hello, this is Daniel from Microsoft Technical Support. We've detected a serious virus on your computer that is sending your personal data to hackers right now. I need you to stay on the line and open a program called AnyDesk so I can remove the virus before it spreads. This is extremely urgent — please don't hang up."

redFlags.note: Microsoft does not proactively call customers about virus detections.

redFlags.snippet: extremely urgent — please don't hang up
redFlags.note: Isolating you and rushing you prevents you from verifying the claim.
Asking you to install remote access software is a major red flag for vishing/tech-support scams.

explanation: This is a vishing (voice phishing) tech-support scam. The caller fabricates an urgent problem and pushes for remote access software, which gives attackers full control of your device.

subject: Quick favor — need this handled discreetly
content:
Hi,

I'm in back-to-back meetings and can't talk right now. I need you to process an urgent wire transfer to a vendor before end of day. Please keep this confidential for now — I'll explain everything once I'm out of meetings.

Let me know once it's done.

Thanks,
Michael
CEO

redFlags.note: Slightly altered version of the real company domain — easy to miss at a glance.
Time pressure discourages verification through normal channels.
Requests for secrecy are designed to prevent you from checking with colleagues.
Impersonating an authority figure pressures the target to comply without question.

explanation: This is Business Email Compromise (BEC) / CEO fraud. It combines a subtly spoofed domain, urgency, secrecy, and authority pressure — a dangerous combination because it specifically targets workplace trust and hierarchy.

subject: Congratulations! You've been selected for a remote position
content:
Dear Applicant,

Congratulations! Based on your profile, you have been selected for a Remote Data Entry position paying $45/hour.

To proceed, please provide your full name, date of birth, Social Security Number, and bank account details so we can set up your payroll and ship your work laptop.

Reply within 48 hours to confirm your position.

HR Department

redFlags.note: Unusually high pay for vague work is a common scam lure.

redFlags.snippet: Social Security Number, and bank account details
redFlags.note: Legitimate employers don't request SSN or banking details before any interview or formal offer.
No personalization, despite claiming to have reviewed your profile.
Forces quick action before the target can research the company.

explanation: This is a fake job offer scam designed to harvest sensitive personal and financial information under the guise of payroll setup.

subject: Bank Alert
content: Chase Fraud Alert: We detected a $1 charge at a gas station. Did you make this purchase? Reply YES or NO. We will never ask for your PIN or password.
explanation: This resembles a legitimate fraud alert. It asks a simple yes/no question, doesn't contain a link, doesn't request sensitive information, and explicitly states the bank will never ask for your PIN or password — a common practice for real bank alerts. Always verify by calling the number on the back of your card if unsure.

subject: Your payment method has expired
content:
Hello,

Your payment method on file has expired. To avoid interruption of your service, please update your billing information within 24 hours.

Update Payment Method: http://netflix-billing-update.com/pay

Thank you,
Netflix Billing Team

redFlags.note: Netflix's real domain is netflix.com — this is a lookalike domain.
Pressure to act fast before verifying.
Link domain does not match the official Netflix domain.

explanation: This is a billing-themed phishing email designed to capture payment card details through a fake update page.

subject: Prescription Refill Reminder
content: "This is an automated call from Riverside Pharmacy. Your prescription is ready for pickup. Our hours are 9 AM to 7 PM, Monday through Saturday. No action is needed if you do not wish to pick it up today. Thank you, and have a great day."
explanation: This is a routine, legitimate automated pharmacy reminder. It doesn't ask for any personal information, doesn't pressure you, and doesn't request any action beyond an optional pickup.

subject: Invoice #88213 Past Due — Action Required
content:
Hello,

Our records show Invoice #88213 ($4,250.00) is now 15 days past due. To avoid late fees and service suspension, please remit payment using the updated banking details below:

Bank: First Continental Trust
Account: 4471-998-2310
Routing: 071000013

Please confirm once payment has been sent.

Regards,
Accounts Receivable

redFlags.note: A sudden change in banking details is one of the strongest signs of invoice fraud.

redFlags.snippet: avoid late fees and service suspension
redFlags.note: Threat of penalty pushes for quick, unverified payment.
No reference to a real contact, project, or prior correspondence.

explanation: This is invoice fraud / vendor email compromise. Attackers impersonate a known vendor and quietly swap in new banking details, redirecting legitimate payments to themselves.

subject: Account Security
content: Apple ID Alert: Your account was used to sign in on a new device near Moscow, Russia. If this wasn't you, secure your account now: appleid-secure-verification.com

redFlags.note: Mentioning a threatening location is designed to trigger panic and quick action.
Apple's real domain is apple.com — this is not an official Apple domain.
Legitimate Apple alerts direct you to sign in via the official Apple ID website or device settings, not an unfamiliar link.

explanation: This is a smishing attack using fear (a foreign login location) to rush victims into entering their Apple ID credentials on a fake site.

subject: You have 3 new messages in #project-alpha
content:
Hi Jordan,

You have 3 unread messages in #project-alpha from the last 24 hours.

Open Slack to catch up, or adjust your notification settings anytime from your preferences.

— The Slack Team
explanation: This is a standard, low-stakes notification email. It's personalized, doesn't request any sensitive information or urgent action, and matches typical legitimate product notification patterns.
